claims.statement: Has shipped a production hybrid retrieval + ranking system to real users at a product company.
claims.conclusion: Directly matches the JD's core must-have: embeddings-based retrieval deployed to real users, not a tutorial demo.

claims.statement: Evaluates ranking systems rigorously with offline NDCG/MRR and online A/B testing.
claims.conclusion: Satisfies the explicit must-have around designing evaluation frameworks for ranking systems.

claims.statement: Strong Python with code-quality signal across assessment, contest, and production repos.
claims.conclusion: Meets the 'strong Python, we care about code quality' bar with corroborating multi-source signal.

claims.statement: Claims LLM fine-tuning (LoRA) but depth is unsubstantiated.
claims.conclusion: Nice-to-have only; the self-listed claim is contradicted by absence of projects, so do not credit fine-tuning depth. Not disqualifying.

claims.statement: Actively available on platform with high recruiter responsiveness.
claims.conclusion: Recently active, 78% recruiter response rate, 30-day notice — genuinely reachable, unlike perfect-on-paper-but-absent candidates.

gaps.note: JD lists LLM fine-tuning as nice-to-have; candidate's claim is unsubstantiated (contradicted), so treat as a gap to probe, not a blocker.
Evidence shows FAISS + OpenSearch/Elasticsearch hybrid, but no managed vector DB; transferable but worth confirming operational depth (index refresh, drift).

uncertainties: Online A/B 14% lift is self-reported on resume and not independently corroborated.
Depth of learning-to-rank modeling (XGBoost) vs. mostly heuristic reranking is unclear from current evidence.
summary: Strong, well-corroborated fit for the search/ranking backend must-haves (production hybrid retrieval, rigorous evaluation, strong Python), genuinely available, with only minor/moderate nice-to-have gaps. The one contradiction (self-listed LLM fine-tuning without projects) is recorded and down-weighted, not subtracted.
